cases.name: Code uses gpa module
cases.description: Your code imports the gpa module and calls the correct functions from it

requirements.name: call gpa_level
requirements.description: Modules must call gpa_level, not define it themselves

cases.name: Code Written to Expectations
cases.description: Your streamlit code is using common streamlit functions taught in the course.

requirements.name: Uses streamlit in streamlit_gpa.py
requirements.description: streamlit_gpa.py imports streamlit and uses button, number_input, title, write.

cases.name: Code style & readability
cases.description: Code is for computers AND humans. Humans should be able to read and understand the code you write. You code should be clearly documented according to python's conventions: black, pylint

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Code is Idiomatic, readable, well factored, well-formatted. Has well placed comments and docstrings in accordance with python linters.

levels.label: Acceptable
levels.description: Code works but has sections that are undocumented, missing docstrings, or are hard to follow the code flow.

levels.label: Unacceptable
levels.description: Code is Unreadable / unstructured. Completely lacking in docstrings, poorly structured.

cases.name: Metacognitive reflection on the work you did
cases.description: Now that you've completed the assignment, think about what you did and share your thoughts. What did you learn? What confuses you? Where did you struggle? Where might you need more practice? A good reflection is as specific as possible, uses the terminology of the problem domain (what was learned in class / through readings), and is actionable (you can pursue next steps, or be aided in the pursuit). You will be judged on those criteria.

levels.label: Excellent
levels.description: Well written, clear, and actionable. Uses terminology of the problem domain. Specific about what was learned and where more practice is needed.

levels.label: Needs Improvement
levels.description: Missing one of: well written, actionable, clear, uses terminology specific to assignment, demonstrated what was learned.
